title: Slab tile pendant set in air-dry clay
subtitle: Three flat clay pendants with stamped designs and pierced cord holes
excerpt: A set of three flat clay pendants cut from a rolled slab, stamped with small designs, and pierced with a cord hole before drying. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over two days, no kiln required. The finished pendants thread onto leather cord or fine chain and are decorative only.
sourceNotes: Slab pendant work is a common entry-point hand-building exercise across studio teaching traditions. Technique derived from first principles. V&A and British Museum open-access historical-amulet collections referenced for general pendant-form study.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and pierce cleanly. It feels firm like thick leather.
Air-dried but unfired. The clay is pale and uniform in colour. For air-dry clay this is the finished structural state.

content.text: A set of three slab pendants is a small project that covers every part of slab work. You roll an even slab. You cut clean shapes. You stamp decoration. You pierce a cord hole at the right stage of drying. You finish the surface. Air-dry clay sets at room temperature over two days. The finished pendants thread onto leather cord or fine chain. The pendants are for show only. Air-dry clay is not waterproof, so do not wear them in the rain or in the shower.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay air-dry for a slightly stronger result

items.name: Two wooden slats (5 mm thick)
items.substitution: Two stacks of two coins each as a guide

items.name: Fettling knife
items.substitution: A craft knife or blunt butter knife

items.name: Pottery stamp or texture mat
items.substitution: A small flat leaf, a piece of lace, or a coin

items.name: Leather cord or fine chain for hanging

content.text: Preparation
Wedge the clay for one or two minutes on the board. The clay should feel uniform throughout with no soft pockets or dry crumbs. A small amount of wedging goes a long way at this scale.
Rolling the slab

Set the two wooden slats parallel on the board, around 12 cm apart. Place the clay ball between them and press flat with the heel of the hand, then roll out with the rolling pin from the centre outward. The slats stop the pin from going thinner than 5 mm and keep the slab even. Roll in one direction, then turn the slab a quarter-turn and roll again. The finished slab is around 12 cm by 8 cm.
Stamping and cutting
1. Stamp first, cut second

Stamp the slab while it is still flat: press the stamp or texture mat firmly into the clay, lift straight up, and leave a clear impression. Stamp three patterns spaced across the slab. Stamping after cutting distorts the pendant shape; stamping first keeps the outline clean.
2. Cut the three shapes

Cut three different shapes around the stamped patterns with the fettling knife. A useful set is a circle 40 mm across, a rectangle 30 mm by 50 mm, and a teardrop 30 mm by 50 mm. Use a small jar lid or a coin as a template for the circle. Round the cut edges by running a damp finger or damp sponge gently around each pendant: a sharp slab edge looks unfinished.
3. Pierce the cord holes

Pierce a hole 6 mm in from the top edge of each pendant using a drinking straw pressed straight through the clay and pulled out. The straw lifts a clean plug; the needle tool alone leaves a torn or oval hole. Aim the hole at the centreline of the pendant, not the corner, so the pendant hangs straight on the cord. Pierce now while the clay is fresh: piercing at
leather-hard
stage is harder and risks cracking the surrounding clay.
Drying

Set the three pendants flat on the board. Cover loosely with a single layer of plastic for the first six hours so the surface and base dry at the same rate. Pendants this thin can curl up at the corners if the top dries faster than the bottom. Turn each pendant over after six hours so both sides air out evenly. Full drying to
bone-dry
takes one to two days at room humidity.
Finishing

Once bone-dry, sand the edges and back of each pendant with 220-grit sandpaper. Do this outdoors or near an open window and wear a mask: dry clay dust is irritating to breathe. Paint with acrylics or leave plain. Rub a darker acrylic into the stamped impressions, then wipe back the surface with a damp sponge: the darker tone stays in the recesses and brings out the stamped pattern. Seal with two thin coats of acrylic varnish brushed on cleanly. Thread each pendant onto a 60 cm length of leather cord or fine chain.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof. The sealed pendants tolerate normal daytime wear but should be removed before showering, swimming, or going out in heavy rain. Wipe clean with a barely damp cloth. Store flat so the cord does not twist.
Variations
Press fresh leaves, flowers, or fine lace into the slab instead of using a stamp. A flat fern leaf gives a clear botanical print.
Cut earrings as a matching pair instead of a third pendant: two small teardrops 25 mm long with a hole pierced at the top of each, threaded onto earring wires.
Use paper clay for a slightly stronger pendant: the fibres reinforce the slab and reduce the risk of snapping at the cord hole.

items.symptom: Cord hole is torn or oval
items.cause: Pierced with a needle alone; the needle tears rather than cuts a clean hole
items.fix: Pierce with a drinking straw pushed straight through and lifted out. The straw cuts a clean plug

items.symptom: Pendant cracks around the cord hole during drying
items.cause: Hole pierced too close to the edge, or pierced after the clay began to firm
items.fix: Pierce at least 6 mm in from the edge while the clay is still fresh; do not pierce a leather-hard pendant

items.symptom: Pendant curls up at the corners as it dries
items.cause: Top dried faster than the bottom
items.fix: Dry under plastic for the first six hours; turn the pendant over after six hours so both faces air out at the same rate

items.symptom: Stamped pattern is faint and unclear
items.cause: Stamp pressed too lightly, or the clay was already starting to firm
items.fix: Press the stamp firmly and decisively, then lift straight up without rocking. Stamp while the slab is freshly rolled
